role_hints.oews_basis: 39-4031 Morticians, Undertakers, and Funeral Arrangers 21.2% of wage bill
role_hints.note: Licensed funeral directors doing arrangement conferences, removals, service direction — relational, physical and legally reserved work. The AI-touchable slice inside the role is paperwork: death certificates, permits, insurance assignments, obituary drafting — meaningful hours but wrapped inside a licensed role.
Often licensee-owners; scheduling, compliance filings, pre-need administration and reporting automate; community presence and family relationships do not.
Visitation staffing, casket movement, processions — physical service work, often part-time.
Licensed physical preparation and cremation operations.

role_hints.oews_basis: 37-3011 Landscaping and Groundskeeping Workers 7.7% — role_mix is 4-digit 8122, which includes 812220 Cemeteries and Crematories; groundskeepers are largely cemetery staff, not funeral-home staff
role_hints.approx_share_of_wage_bill: ~8% in the proxy; lower at 812210 proper
role_hints.note: Strip cemetery grounds labor when modeling funeral-home-only wage structure; combo firms (funeral home + cemetery) do exist.
Case administration, filings, billing, aftercare correspondence, phone handling — the clean AI surface; small base.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Representatives of Services 2.7% + unlisted tail (drivers, support)
role_hints.note: Pre-need selling is relationship/trust work with heavy paperwork; removal/transport driving is physical.

source_hints.source: NFDA (National Funeral Directors Association) member surveys and workforce reports
source_hints.why: Owner demographics, succession-plan data and median revenue/case metrics for the family-owned base.

source_hints.source: NFDA General Price List survey (median cost of funeral) and Cremation & Burial Report (annual)
source_hints.why: The standard pricing and cremation-rate data — the revenue-per-case trajectory that dominates this industry's economics.

source_hints.source: FTC Funeral Rule (16 CFR Part 453) and its ongoing review proceedings on online price disclosure
source_hints.why: The statutory pricing-disclosure mechanism (itemized GPL); a rule change forcing online price posting would compress local price opacity.

source_hints.source: CANA (Cremation Association of North America) annual statistics report
source_hints.why: Cremation-rate projections by state — the demand-mix variable that outweighs any technology effect.

source_hints.source: Johnson Consulting Group (funeral and cemetery M&A advisory) commentary
source_hints.why: The specialist sell-side advisor; source for prevailing multiples on $1-10M EBITDA funeral businesses.

source_hints.source: Service Corporation International (NYSE: SCI) and Park Lawn Corporation filings
source_hints.why: Public consolidator disclosures: revenue per case, cremation mix effects, acquisition multiples and pre-need trust economics.

capture_questions: Pricing is consumer-paid, itemized under the FTC Funeral Rule's General Price List, with strong local price opacity and grief-constrained shopping — cost savings from AI admin are retained margin by default; but the controlling revenue question is mix: as cremation share rises and direct-cremation entrants advertise transparent low prices online, does average revenue per case erode faster than any cost line AI can save? Quantify revenue-per-burial vs revenue-per-cremation from NFDA/SCI data.

The moat claim is the family name and multi-generational community trust: what evidence exists on case-volume retention after an acquisition where the family name is retained but the selling director exits (SCI/Park Lawn practice), versus independent competitors advertising 'family-owned' against consolidator-held homes?

What can bypass the funeral home: online direct-cremation brands (e.g. Tulip, Solace) that never use the acquired firm's premises, DIY/home funerals (legal in most states), and celebration-of-life venues that unbundle the service from the disposition — how big and how fast-growing is the bypassed share of cases?

Pre-need books: trust- or insurance-funded pre-need contracts lock future volume but at locked-in prices with regulated trust accounting — in an acquisition, is the pre-need book an asset (guaranteed volume) or a liability (below-inflation locked pricing, servicing obligations), and who keeps the trust investment income under state rules?
terminal_value_question: Funeral service is licensed, physical and relational — mature AI does not embalm, transport, conduct services or sit with grieving families; the automatable surface is the ~11% office/admin wage share plus paperwork hours inside licensed roles. The mechanism that actually moves terminal value is not AI but the cremation mix shift: direct cremation generates a fraction of traditional burial revenue per case, cremation share keeps climbing, and online direct-cremation entrants disintermediate the physical premises entirely — so the sharp question is whether an AI-cost story adds anything material on top of a business whose case volume grows demographically while revenue per case shrinks structurally. A researcher who scores 'deaths are increasing, demand is safe' without modeling revenue-per-case decline has failed this check.
special_notes: Cremation-mix deflation is the dominant economic force: US cremation rate is past 60% and rising (CANA/NFDA projections), and a direct cremation can be sold at one-third or less of a traditional burial's revenue — a secular revenue-per-case decline independent of AI; never let 'growing death rate' stand in for revenue growth.

EBITDA normalization: family owner comp (often multiple family members on payroll), owner-held real estate at non-market rent, personal vehicles, and pre-need trust investment income (non-operating, state-regulated) all sit inside small-firm P&Ls — normalize all four before quoting margins or multiples; the n_band chain's generic 15.65% margin is below the 20-25%+ EBITDA margins healthy funeral homes often run, so n_band 40 (only the 100-499 class) likely understates the band — parts of the 893-firm 20-99 class (avg revenue $4.3M) would qualify at realistic margins; treat 40 as a floor.

Sibling contamination: role_mix is 4-digit 8122 including 812220 Cemeteries and Crematories (hence 7.7% groundskeepers) — strip cemetery occupations for funeral-home-only structure; also note combo (funeral home + cemetery) operators straddle both codes and SCI reports them together.

Regulatory: state funeral director/embalmer licensing everywhere; several states impose funeral-establishment ownership or licensee-in-charge requirements, and some (notably licensing-board-restrictive states) constrain corporate ownership — check the state pattern for roll-up friction; the FTC Funeral Rule review (potential mandatory online price posting) is a live pricing-transparency risk.

Pre-need accounting: pre-need sales create deferred revenue and trust obligations under state law — reported revenue timing differs between at-need and pre-need heavy firms; quality of earnings must separate them.

Consolidators (SCI ~15-16% of US revenue, Park Lawn, Foundation Partners, Legacy Funeral Group, plus quiet family-office buyers; Johnson Consulting brokered) — B/M evidence only, never V/A (rule 2); note SCI's scale has NOT collapsed the fragmented independent base, itself informative for B.
